coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-based yellow-emitting carbon dots for fluorescence-smartphone dual-mode detection of vitamin B6 in milk
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chenzhao
Wang, Xiujuan
Jiang, Yuhao
Wu, Zhiyu
Yang, Jiawei
Wei, Shanshan
Wang, Zhibing
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: The determination of vitamin B6 (VB6) in food is of great significance due to its vital role in maintaining health and its necessity for ingestion through dietary sources. Therefore, based on ionic liquid-based yellow-emitting carbon dots (Y-CDs), a novel fluorescence-smartphone dual-mode method was first developed. The present method was applied to the detection of VB6 in milk. In the fluorescence method, the formation of complexes between VB6 and Y-CDs results in a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs. VB6 in milk samples was successfully determined according to this method, which exhibited a low detection limit (5 × 10−5 mg/mL) and excellent recoveries (98.80%–103.80%), demonstrating its feasibility in real sample analysis. In addition, the smartphone-based analysis method was established by researching the correlation between different VB6 concentrations and the (R + B) values of Y-CDs. When this method was applied, the detection process of VB6 was simplified. By combining the two methods, the possibility of incorrect analysis results can be effectively reduced, and the reliability of detection results can be improved through cross-validation of the two methods. Compared with traditional chromatography and electrochemical methods, the dual-mode method was more rapid, convenient, accurate, and suitable for the detection of VB6.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vitamin B6
Ionic liquid
Carbon dots
Dual-mode
Milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM and HR-TEM image, (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of the Y-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the Y-CDs. Inset: pictures under white light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra with the excitation wavelength increasing from 430 to 490 nm.

(A) Lifetimes of the Y-CDs and Y-CDs + VB6. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of VB6 and fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs, VB6, and Y-CDs + VB6. (D) Three stern-volmer curves at different temperatures (303 K,313 K, and 323 K).
(A) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of VB6 (0–10 mg/mL). (B) Effect of different interfering substances and VB6 on Y-CDs.
(A) The process of smartphone photo analysis. (B) The relationship between R + B values and different concentrations of VB6 (0–10 mg/mL).
Preparation and application of the Y-CDs.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-based yellow-emitting carbon dots for fluorescence-smartphone dual-mode detection of vitamin B6 in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination of vitamin B6 (VB6) in food is of great significance due to its vital role in maintaining health and its necessity for ingestion through dietary sources. Therefore, based on ionic liquid-based yellow-emitting carbon dots (Y-CDs), a novel fluorescence-smartphone dual-mode method was first developed. The present method was applied to the detection of VB6 in milk. In the fluorescence method, the formation of complexes between VB6 and Y-CDs results in a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs. VB6 in milk samples was successfully determined according to this method, which exhibited a low detection limit (5 × 10−5 mg/mL) and excellent recoveries (98.80%–103.80%), demonstrating its feasibility in real sample analysis. In addition, the smartphone-based analysis method was established by researching the correlation between different VB6 concentrations and the (R + B) values of Y-CDs. When this method was applied, the detection process of VB6 was simplified. By combining the two methods, the possibility of incorrect analysis results can be effectively reduced, and the reliability of detection results can be improved through cross-validation of the two methods. Compared with traditional chromatography and electrochemical methods, the dual-mode method was more rapid, convenient, accurate, and suitable for the detection of VB6.

Preparation and application of the Y-CDs.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B6 (VB6) is an essential organic compound for maintaining overall health, promoting metabolism, supporting growth and development, and enhancing immunity (Hellmann & Mooney, 2010). It plays a crucial role in combating neuritis, alleviating drug-induced nausea and vomiting, and preventing infant anemia (Hajsl et al., 2020). Prolonged deficiency of VB6 can lead to physiological dysfunctions and various symptoms such as dermatitis, anemia, insomnia, weakness in limbs, and sudden spasms (Ueland et al., 2017). Since the human body cannot synthesize VB6 independently, it must be obtained through dietary intake (di Salvo et al., 2011). By accurately determining VB6 levels, it is possible to assess the nutritional value of food products to ensure that consumers are getting enough VB6 and also to prevent health problems caused by excessive levels. In addition, it can help manufacturers monitor production processes to ensure that products meet standards, and support researchers in optimizing product formulations and improving product quality. Therefore, the establishment of a rapid, accurate, and sensitive VB6 detection method is of great significance for evaluating the efficacy and safety of food and pharmaceuticals.

Currently, various methods have been developed for the determination of VB6 and possess unique advantages. For example, chromatography is characterized by its high accuracy and wide applicability and can meet the detection requirements of different sample types (Schmidt et al., 2017). Mass spectrometry is known for its high sensitivity, which enables the detection of low concentrations of VB6 (Sentkowska et al., 2020). Electrochemical methods typically offer faster detection speeds and are suitable for real-time monitoring and rapid analysis (Ganjali et al., 2011). However, these methods also face common challenges, including high equipment and maintenance costs, the requirement for specialized operational skills and data analysis capabilities, and complex sample pretreatment steps. It is noteworthy that fluorometric assays have attracted considerable attention for their simplicity, affordability, high sensitivity, selectivity, rapid detection, and real-time monitoring capabilities (Jun et al., 2020). Yue et al. achieved highly selective and sensitive detection of VB6 using a zinc-based metal-organic framework as a fluorescent probe (Yue et al., 2024). Chauhan et al. applied red-emitting bovine serum albumin-stabilized copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) to the specific detection of VB6 and nitroaromatics (Chauhan et al., 2021). However, these fluorescent materials are expensive to synthesize and exhibit instability, susceptibility to oxidation and contamination, and lack of biological compatibility, which limit their application in food and biological samples. Consequently, it remains a challenge to develop a cost-effective, structurally stable, and user-friendly fluorescent probe that can achieve highly sensitive detection of VB6 in complex matrices.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of nanomaterial with a diameter of <10 nm (Li, Chen, Zhao, Vomiero, & Gong, 2023; Liu et al., 2017). They demonstrate exceptional optical properties and outstanding fluorescence performance (Wei et al., 2020). Additionally, they exhibit remarkable biocompatibility and low toxicity to biological tissues (Wang, Wang, et al., 2023). These characterizations make them highly promising for wide applications in food analysis. In particular, CDs prepared from ionic liquids exhibit superior performance (Zhang et al., 2020). Compared with common CDs, ionic liquid-based carbon dots (IL-CDs) possess the capability to enhance their fluorescence quantum yield and adjust the emission wavelength through modulation of the carbon chain length of ionic liquids (Zheng et al., 2019). Additionally, the chemical and physical properties of ionic liquids can enhance the biocompatibility, fluorescence responsiveness, and chemical activity of CDs (Guo et al., 2022). These advantages make IL-CDs have broad application potential in catalysis, drug delivery, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and analytical sensing (Shu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, the development of fluorescent probes based on IL-CDs will provide a more stable and efficient tool for the detection of VB6 in food.

Smartphone-based photo analysis plays an increasingly important role in fluorescence analysis (Mu et al., 2022). Compared to traditional detection instruments, smartphone-based photo analysis offers lower detection costs and a more convenient detection process. Additionally, smartphones enable real-time data collection and analysis, which can effectively reduce detection time (Wang, Li, et al., 2023). Based on the advantages of IL-CDs and smartphone-based photo analysis, yellow-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (Y-CDs) were developed for determining the concentration of VB6 in milk (Scheme 1 ). VB6 can interact with Y-CDs through a static quenching mechanism, leading to a reduction of Y-CDs fluorescence. Based on the change in fluorescence colors of Y-CDs exposed to different concentrations of VB6, real-time and rapid detection of VB6 was achieved using smartphone photo analysis. This approach not only exhibits higher sensitivity and stability but also achieves dual-mode detection of VB6 in complex matrices. Moreover, it provides more reliable technical support for food quality monitoring and safety and offers new materials and ideas for the effective analysis of food constituents.

Vitamin B6 (VB6), Vitamin A (VA), Vitamin C (VC), Vitamin B2 (VB2), Vitamin B9 (VB9) and Vitamin B12 (VB12) were provided by Shanghai Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-Octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water was prepared using a laboratory water purification system (HHitech, Master Touch-S15UVF). Reagents (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, and F−) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Various milk samples were bought from local supermarkets (Changchun, China).

The size and morphology of Y-CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100F, JEOL) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) of Y-CDs were collected by an X-ray diffractometer (Smart Lab, Rigaku). The functional groups of Y-CDs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet IS50, Thermofisher). Elemental content was obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (X‘Pert3 MRD, Malvern Panalytical). Fluorescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer (FluroroMax Plus, Horiba), while UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UVmini-1280, Shimadzu). The prepared Y-CDs were freeze-dried by a freeze dryer (LGJ-10, Foring) and stored at −4 °C.

Firstly, 0.5 g of OPD and 2 g of [OMIM]BF4 were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and placed in a 50 mL reaction flask. Subsequently, the resulting solution was heated at 180 °C for 12 h in an oven and cooled to room temperature after reaction. Finally, the prepared Y-CDs were dialyzed in deionized water for 10 h with a dialysis bag (MW 500 Da).

The 200 μL of Y-CDs were mixed with 200 μL of standard solutions of VB6 at various concentrations, and the total volume was adjusted to 3 mL with ethanol. The concentration of VB6 in the mixed solution ranged from 0 to 10 mg/mL, and the fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs were recorded under excitation at 480 nm. According to the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs and the concentration of VB6, the sensitivity, linear range and limit of detection (LOD) of this method was obtained. The sensitivity was determined by calculating the slope of the calibration curve, with a measured value of 2.54 × 105 a.u./(mg/mL). The linear range was identified by the lowest and highest VB concentrations. The LOD was determined by calculating the mean plus three times the standard deviation of 20 blank samples. Additionally, the selectivity of Y-CDs was investigated using the same method for determining some ions and compounds, including Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, F−, VA, VC, VB2, VB9 and VB12.

Firstly, 20 mL of milk samples from different brands were put in centrifuge tubes and treated with chloroform to prepare milk sample solutions after centrifugation and filtration. Then, 200 μL of Y-CDs solution was mixed with 200 μL of the milk sample solution and adjusted to a total volume of 3 mL with ethanol. The mixture was poured into a cuvette, which was subsequently placed in a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was recorded at a wavelength of 480 nm.

Different concentrations of VB6 solution with Y-CDs were added to the cuvette and placed in a fixed position in a dark environment. After 1 min of reaction, the cuvette was placed under UV light to take a photo. The fluorescence photographs were captured and the RGB values of the photographs were extracted by the application of smartphone (Color Picker). The linear relationship between the (R + B) value and the concentration of VB6 was calculated. The concentration of VB6 in real samples was further determined.

The TEM was employed for characterization to investigate the size and morphology of Y-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, Y-CDs exhibited uniformly dispersed spherical particles with an average diameter of 1.84 ± 0.44 nm. The particle size distribution was concentrated in the range of 1.0 to 3.5 nm with a normal distribution trend. HR-TEM images of Y-CDs illustrated multiple lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.19 nm (Fig. 1B). In addition, Y-CDs exhibited a broad and intense diffraction peak near 2θ = 21.4° corresponding to the graphite structure of 002, which suggested the presence of abundant crystalline graphite in Y-CDs (Fig. 1C) (Xiong et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 1D, the IR spectra of Y-CDs showed different vibrational peaks from ILs, which provided further evidence for the formation of Y-CDs. After the formation of Y-CDs, the broad peaks at 3421 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH and CH bonds, respectively (Han et al., 2016). The absorption peak at 1580 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the CC double bond (Zhang et al., 2023). The stretching vibrations of the CF and CO bonds were attributed to the sharp peaks at 1304 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1, respectively (Uriarte et al., 2021).

The electronic structure and surface elemental composition of the Y-CDs were determined by XPS. The XPS spectra exhibited five peaks corresponding to the elements C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, F 1 s, and B 1 s, respectively (Fig. S1A). Further analysis indicated the respective elemental contents of C (65.99%), N (9.35%), O (7.14%), F (12.81%), and B (4.71%). Specifically, the C 1 s spectrum exhibited peaks of CC, CN, and CO bonds at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 289.1 eV (Fig. S1B) (Liao et al., 2015). Two peaks were observed in the N 1 s spectrum at 399.3 eV and 401.8 eV, corresponding to CN and NH structures, respectively (Fig. S1C) (Ma et al., 2020). In the O 1 s spectrum, two peaks at 531.9 eV and 532.7 eV were ascribed to CO and CO structures, respectively (Fig. S1D) (Shi et al., 2017). The peaks of the F 1 s spectrum at 685.3 eV and 686.0 eV were attributed to the half-ionic and covalent bonding CF characteristic peaks (Fig. S1E). Simultaneously, the B 1 s spectrum exhibited peaks at 193.7 eV and 194.2 eV corresponding to BC and BO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1F) (Huang et al., 2020). These results indicated that the atoms of N, F, and B were successfully doped into the Y-CDs, which contributed to the enhancement of their fluorescence properties.

The fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs were analyzed to investigate the optical properties of Y-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2A, the absorption peak of Y-CDs at 292 nm was attributed to n-π* transitions of C=O/C-N structures (de Haan et al., 2021). Additionally, Y-CDs exhibited a strong absorption peak at 432 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions of CC bonds. As depicted in Fig. 2B, different emission wavelengths of Y-CDs were recorded when the excitation wavelengths ranged from 430 to 490 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of Y-CDs was found at 547 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. And the emission spectra exhibited no redshift with increasing excitation wavelength, which demonstrated the good excitation independence of Y-CDs. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of Y-CDs was determined to be 20.86% using a transient fluorescence spectrometer with an average fluorescence lifetime of 0.12 ns (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was relatively stable in the pH range of 4.0–13.0. Under acidic or alkaline conditions, protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. In addition, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was studied by changing the NaCl concentration. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs slightly decreased as the NaCl concentration increased from 10−7 to 1.0 M. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the Y-CDs did not significantly change after being placed under UV light for 10 min (Fig. S5). Finally, the effect of temperature on the Y-CDs was researched, and the results indicated that the Y-CDs maintained high fluorescence intensity even at a high temperature (80 °C). In summary, the prepared Y-CDs possessed excellent pH stability, salt stability, and photobleaching resistance, which ensured their reliability and durability for applications in sensing and bioimaging.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was explored by measuring the fluorescence lifetimes, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Y-CDs remained almost unchanged after the addition of VB6, which excluded the possibility of dynamic quenching and Förster resonance energy transfer (Fig. 3A). Therefore, the quenching mechanism between Y-CDs and VB6 could be static quenching or internal filtering effect. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, there is no significant overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of VB6 and the excitation and emission spectra of Y-CDs, which ruled out the possibility of inner filtering effect. Furthermore, Fig. 3C demonstrated that a new absorption peak at 450 nm appeared in the UV absorption spectrum of Y-CDs after the addition of VB6, which indicated that the interaction between Y-CDs and VB6 had occurred. The fluorescence quenching constant between Y-CDs and VB6 could be calculated using the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation.
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
Q
where F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs, while F represented the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs after the addition of VB6. Ksv denoted the quenching constant between Y-CDs and VB6, and [Q] represented the concentration of VB6. The fluorescence quenching constants from the Stern-Volmer curves gradually decrease with increasing temperature (303 K, 313 K, and 323 K). And the Ksv values at the three temperatures were 0.522, 0.489, and 0.486 mL/mg, respectively, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was static quenching.

Under optimized conditions, the linear range of the method was determined to be 0–10 mg/mL, and the LOD was calculated to be 5 × 10−5 mg/mL using the experimental methods described in Section 2.4. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of VB6 concentration. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs and the concentration of VB6 was described by the regression eq. Y = exp. (−0.18×) (R2 = 0.9959) in the range of 0–10 mg/mL. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 5 × 10−5 mg/mL by adding three times the average standard deviation to the mean of 20 blank samples. The LOD of this method is much lower than the concentrations of VB6 usually found in milk samples (Schmidt et al., 2017; Scott & Bishop, 1986; Shetty et al., 2020; Vincenzetti et al., 2020), making it suitable for the determination of VB6 in milk. Compared with other methods for VB6 detection, this method exhibited a lower limit of detection and a wider range of detection (Table S1). The present method is suitable for the analysis of food samples.

The fluorescence intensity changes of Y-CDs were examined in the presence of various interfering substances (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, F−, VA, VC, VB2, VB9 and VB12). As shown in Fig. 4B, there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs in the presence of various interfering substances. However, compared with other substances, VB6 notably quenched the fluorescence signal of Y-CDs. The experimental results indicated that Y-CDs possessed excellent selectivity for the detection of VB6.

Five milk samples were selected as real samples to verify the feasibility of Y-CDs for the detection of VB6. Initially, the comparison of the standard curve method with the working curve method revealed no significant difference, indicating the absence of matrix effects. Therefore, the standard curve method was chosen for the real sample detection. Different concentrations of VB6 (0.1, 1.0, and 5.0 mg/mL) were added to the sample solution, and each concentration was performed six times (Table S2). The spiked recoveries of VB6 in the five milk samples ranged from 98.80% to 103.80%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) between 0.32% and 2.97%. These results indicated that the method could quantitatively detect the content of VB6 in food with excellent accuracy and precision.

In order to realize the rapid and real-time detection of VB6, the real samples were analyzed by the smartphone-based photo analysis technique. Fig. 5A and B illustrated a good linear relationship between the R + B values and VB6 concentration (0–10 mg/mL): R + B = −17.45 [VB6] + 351.59 (R2 = 0.9978). The LOD was calculated as 6.2 × 10−4 mg/mL using the 3σ/K method, and the results of real samples exhibited excellent recoveries (Table S3). Where σ represented the standard deviation of the response values from 11 blank samples, and K denoted the slope of the linear curve. A t-test was performed on the sample data in Sections 3.6 and 3.7, and statistical analysis showed that there was no significant difference between the two groups at the 95% confidence level (t = 1.698 < 2.145). Therefore, smartphone-based photo analysis demonstrated its rapid, convenient, and feasible application in detecting VB6 from samples. More significantly, the dual-mode approach (fluorescence and smartphone) enabled high-sensitivity detection of VB6 and mutual support to ensure the accuracy of the detection results.

In summary, Y-CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method using [OMIM]PF4 as the raw material for the detection of VB6 in milk. The Y-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence stability as well as good selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of VB6. Furthermore, satisfactory results were obtained for milk sample analysis in both fluorescence and smartphone detection. Compared with other methods, the dual-mode detection enhanced the stability and accuracy of VB6 detection and also realized the on-site and real-time detection of VB6. It not only provides high-performance fluorescent materials for food analysis but also offers a new solution for the rapid and accurate detection of VB6.

Chenzhao Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiujuan Wang: Validation, Methodology. Yuhao Jiang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhiyu Wu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiawei Yang: Validation, Methodology. Shanshan Wei: Validation, Methodology. Zhibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This wok was financially supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC), Science and Technology Research Fund for Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (No. 20190304011YY) and Department of Education of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20210762KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.140525.
